From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Test Act](/w/index.php?title=Test_Act&redirect=no "Test Act"))

1673 series of English penal laws

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/A_Word_of_Comfort2.jpg/250px-A_Word_of_Comfort2.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Word_of_Comfort2.jpg)

A 1790 cartoon satirizing the efforts of [Charles James Fox](/wiki/Charles_James_Fox "Charles James Fox") and [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") theologian [Joseph Priestley](/wiki/Joseph_Priestley "Joseph Priestley") to get the Test Acts repealed. Priestly preaches from atop a pile of his own works, in a pulpit inscribed "FANATICISM", to Fox seated in a box pew. Fox asks, "Pray, Doctor is there such a thing as a Devil?" Priestley responds "No", however [the devil](/wiki/Satan "Satan") himself announces, "If you had eyes behind, you'd know better my dear Doctor".

The **Test Acts** were a series of [penal laws](/wiki/Penal_law_(British) "Penal law (British)") originating in [Restoration England](/wiki/Restoration_England "Restoration England"), passed by the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"), that served as a religious test for [public office](/wiki/Public_office "Public office") in [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), which imposed various [civil disabilities](/wiki/Civil_disabilities "Civil disabilities") on [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church_in_England_and_Wales "Roman Catholic Church in England and Wales") and [nonconformist Protestants](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)"). The underlying principle was that only people taking [communion](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") in the established [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") were eligible for public employment, and the severe penalties pronounced against [recusants](/wiki/Recusants "Recusants"), whether Catholic or nonconformist, were affirmations of this principle.[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1)

Although theoretically encompassing all who refuse to comply with Anglicanism in a dragnet approach, in practice the nonconformist Protestants had many defenders in Parliament and were often exempted from some of these laws through the regular passage of [Acts of Indemnity](/wiki/Act_of_Indemnity#United_Kingdom_and_preceding_states "Act of Indemnity"): in particular, the [Indemnity Act 1727](/wiki/Indemnity_Act_1727 "Indemnity Act 1727") relieved nonconformists from the requirements in the [Test Act 1673](/wiki/Test_Act_1673 "Test Act 1673") and the [Corporation Act 1661](/wiki/Corporation_Act_1661 "Corporation Act 1661") that public office holders must have taken the [sacrament of the Lord's Supper](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") in an Anglican church.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

An exception was at [Oxbridge](/wiki/Oxbridge "Oxbridge"), where nonconformists and Catholics could not matriculate (Oxford) or graduate (Cambridge) until 1871.

Similar laws were introduced in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") with respect to the [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity") [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") and also in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), where the minority Anglican [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") had [penal laws](/wiki/Penal_laws_(Ireland) "Penal laws (Ireland)") set up in its favour to allow the [Anglo-Irish](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_people "Anglo-Irish people") minority to maintain control of land, law and politics as part of the [Protestant Ascendancy](/wiki/Protestant_Ascendancy "Protestant Ascendancy").

## Corporation Act 1661

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Test_Acts_1673_%26_1678&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Corporation Act 1661")]

Main article: [Corporation Act 1661](/wiki/Corporation_Act_1661 "Corporation Act 1661")

The [Naturalisation and Restoration of Blood Act 1609](/w/index.php?title=Naturalisation_and_Restoration_of_Blood_Act_1609&action=edit&redlink=1 "Naturalisation and Restoration of Blood Act 1609 (page does not exist)") ([7 Jas. 1](/wiki/7_Jas._1 "7 Jas. 1"). c. 2) provided that all such as were naturalised or restored in blood should receive the sacrament of the [Lord's Supper](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") (repealed by [Naturalization Act 1870](/wiki/Naturalization_Act_1870 "Naturalization Act 1870") ([33 & 34 Vict.](/wiki/33_%26_34_Vict. "33 & 34 Vict.") c. 14)) but this did not affect most people born in England.

It was not, however, until the reign of [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") that actually receiving [communion](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") was made a precondition for holding [public office](/wiki/Public_office "Public office"). The earliest imposition of this test was by the [Corporation Act 1661](/wiki/Corporation_Act_1661 "Corporation Act 1661") ([13 Cha. 2 St. 2](/wiki/13_Cha._2_St._2 "13 Cha. 2 St. 2"). c. 1) requiring that, besides taking the [Oath of Supremacy](/wiki/Oath_of_Supremacy "Oath of Supremacy"), all members of corporations (i.e. municipal authorities) were, within one year after election, to receive the sacrament of the Lord's Supper according to the rites of the Church of England. This was targeted at Presbyterians and other non-conformists following the English Civil War and Restoration, though also affected Roman Catholics.[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1)

## Test Act 1673

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Test_Acts_1673_%26_1678&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Test Act 1673")]

The [Corporation Act 1661](/wiki/Corporation_Act_1661 "Corporation Act 1661") ([13 Cha. 2 St. 2](/wiki/13_Cha._2_St._2 "13 Cha. 2 St. 2"). c. 1) was followed by the **Test Act 1673**[[3]](#cite_note-4) ([25 Cha. 2](/wiki/25_Cha._2 "25 Cha. 2"). c. 2) (the [long title](/wiki/Long_title "Long title") of which is "An act for preventing dangers which may happen from popish recusants").[[4]](#cite_note-5) This act enforced upon all persons filling any office, civil, military or religious, the obligation of taking the oaths of supremacy and allegiance and subscribing to a declaration against [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation") and also of receiving the sacrament within three months after admittance to office.[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1) The oath for the Test Act 1673 was:

> I, *N*, do declare that I do believe that there is not any transubstantiation in the sacrament of the Lord's Supper, or in the elements of the [bread and wine](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament"), at or after the [consecration](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration") thereof by any person whatsoever.

The act was passed in the parliamentary session that began on 4 February 1673 ([Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar")). The act is, however, dated 1672 in some accounts due to the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") then in force in England.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

One of the immediate reasons that the "Country Party" (proto-Whigs) in Parliament pushed for this was to break up the [Cabal ministry](/wiki/Cabal_ministry "Cabal ministry") — members of the Court Party of powerful statement under [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), who had divergent religious interests — the Catholic [Lord Clifford](/wiki/Thomas_Clifford,_1st_Baron_Clifford_of_Chudleigh "Thomas Clifford, 1st Baron Clifford of Chudleigh") could not accept this oath which ran contrary to his beliefs, so resigned his position in government and the Cabal ministry completely unravelled by 1674. Foreign nations and their agents also had a vested interest in lobbying either way on the issue, as the leaders of the Cabal ministry (Arlington and Clifford) were allied with Catholic France against the Protestant Dutch in the [Third Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Dutch_War "Third Anglo-Dutch War"); after the fall of the Cabal ministry, the pro-Dutch [First Danby ministry](/wiki/First_Danby_ministry "First Danby ministry") came to power. In addition to this, 1673 was also the year that it became public knowledge that [James, Duke of York](/wiki/James,_Duke_of_York "James, Duke of York"), heir to the throne, had converted to Catholicism.

The whole act was repealed by section 1 of, and the schedule to, the [Statute Law Revision Act 1863](/wiki/Statute_Law_Revision_Act_1863 "Statute Law Revision Act 1863") ([26 & 27 Vict.](/wiki/26_%26_27_Vict. "26 & 27 Vict.") c. 125).

## Test Act 1678

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Test_Acts_1673_%26_1678&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Test Act 1678")]

Initially, the act did not extend to [peers](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), but in 1678 the act was extended by a further act, the **Parliament Act 1678** ([30 Cha. 2. St. 2](/wiki/30_Cha._2._St._2 "30 Cha. 2. St. 2")),[[6]](#cite_note-8) which required that all peers and members of the [House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons") should make a declaration against transubstantiation, invocation of saints, and the sacrificial nature of the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)").[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1) The effect of this was to exclude [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") from both houses, and in particular the "[Five Popish Lords](/wiki/Five_popish_lords "Five popish lords")" from the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), a change motivated largely by the alleged [Popish Plot](/wiki/Popish_Plot "Popish Plot"). The Lords deeply resented this interference with their membership; they delayed passage of the act as long as possible, and managed to greatly weaken it by including an exemption for the future [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), effective head of the Catholic nobility, at whom it was largely aimed.[[7]](#cite_note-9)

## Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Test_Acts_1673_%26_1678&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Scotland")]

In Scotland, a religious test was imposed immediately after the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), and by the [Religion Act 1567](/w/index.php?title=Religion_Act_1567&action=edit&redlink=1 "Religion Act 1567 (page does not exist)") (April c. 2 (S)) no one was to be appointed to a public office or to be a [notary](/wiki/Notary "Notary") who did not profess [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"). The Scottish Test Act (c. 6 (S)) was passed in 1681 but rescinded by the [Confession of Faith Ratification Act 1690](/w/index.php?title=Confession_of_Faith_Ratification_Act_1690&action=edit&redlink=1 "Confession of Faith Ratification Act 1690 (page does not exist)"). Later attempts to exclude Scotland from the English Test Acts were rejected by the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland"). In 1707, anyone bearing office in any university, college or school in Scotland was to profess and subscribe to the [Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith"). All persons were to be free of any oath or test contrary to or inconsistent with the Protestant religion and Presbyterian Church government. The reception of the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") was never a part of the test in Scotland as it was in England and Ireland. The necessity for subscription to the Confession of Faith by persons holding a university office was removed by the [Universities (Scotland) Act 1853](/wiki/Universities_(Scotland)_Act_1853 "Universities (Scotland) Act 1853"). The act provided that in place of subscription every person appointed to a university office was to subscribe a declaration according to the form in the act, promising not to teach any opinions opposed to the divine authority of Scripture or to the Confession of Faith, and to do nothing to the prejudice of the Church of Scotland or its doctrines and privileges.[[8]](#cite_note-10) All tests were finally abolished by the [Universities (Scotland) Act 1889](/wiki/Universities_(Scotland)_Act_1889 "Universities (Scotland) Act 1889").[[9]](#cite_note-11)[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1)

The necessity of receiving the sacrament as a qualification for office was repealed in Ireland in 1780 by the [Protestant Dissenters Relief Act 1779](/w/index.php?title=Protestant_Dissenters_Relief_Act_1779&action=edit&redlink=1 "Protestant Dissenters Relief Act 1779 (page does not exist)") ([19 & 20 Geo. 3](/wiki/19_%26_20_Geo._3_(I) "19 & 20 Geo. 3 (I)"). c. 6 (I)),[[10]](#cite_note-13)[[11]](#cite_note-TM-14) and by the [Sacramental Test Act 1828](/wiki/Sacramental_Test_Act_1828 "Sacramental Test Act 1828") in England and Wales. Provisions requiring the taking of oaths and declarations against [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation") were repealed by the [Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Relief_Act_1829 "Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829").[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1) Sir [Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel") took the lead for the government in the repeal and collaborated with Anglican Church leaders.[[12]](#cite_note-15) The application of the 1828 and 1829 acts to Irish acts was uncertain and so the **Test Abolition Act 1867** ([30 & 31 Vict.](/wiki/30_%26_31_Vict. "30 & 31 Vict.") c. 62) repeated the 1829 repeal more explicitly.[[13]](#cite_note-16)

The 1661, 1672 and 1678 acts were repealed by the [Promissory Oaths Act 1871](/wiki/Promissory_Oaths_Act_1871 "Promissory Oaths Act 1871"), the [Statute Law Revision Act 1863](/wiki/Statute_Law_Revision_Act_1863 "Statute Law Revision Act 1863") and the [Parliamentary Oaths Act 1866](/w/index.php?title=Parliamentary_Oaths_Act_1866&action=edit&redlink=1 "Parliamentary Oaths Act 1866 (page does not exist)") respectively.[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1) Religious tests for officers of the [ancient universities](/wiki/Ancient_universities "Ancient universities") were repealed by the [Universities Tests Act 1871](/wiki/Universities_Tests_Act_1871 "Universities Tests Act 1871") for England, the [University of Dublin Tests Act 1873](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Dublin_Tests_Act_1873&action=edit&redlink=1 "University of Dublin Tests Act 1873 (page does not exist)"), and the [Universities (Scotland) Act 1889](/wiki/Universities_(Scotland)_Act_1889 "Universities (Scotland) Act 1889").[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1)
